texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Abstract
texts.text: Abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) whether of the DCMD (direct contact membrane distillation) or VMD (vacuum membrane distillation) variety can have a role to play in desalting highly saline waters that have considerable osmotic pressures where reverse osmosis (RO) operation becomes more expensive and problematic. Using MD in this way would allow increased recovery and help reduce the problem of concentrate disposal vexing inland desalination. To realize this promise, MD must show itself to be more resistant to scaling than RO and thus not limited by it in the way that RO is. An analysis of the scaling potential in hollow fiber membrane-based crossflow DCMD is presented in terms of the saturation index profiles throughout the hollow fiber membrane module as a function of the location in the module for the sparingly soluble salt, CaSO4, in various forms (such as, gypsum and anhydrite). Modeling shows that the highest scaling potential is to be found at the high temperature end of the module both due to the high brine temperature and concentration polarization associated with high local fluxes. Concentration effects are far more important than temperature, although concentration polarization estimated in crossflow hollow fiber DCMD units is lower than that in spiral wound modules in RO for similar flux values. Experimental results at high saturation indices on the calcium sulfate system ranging between 1.13 and 1.93 show that despite evidence that calcium sulfate precipitates at elevated temperatures, no significant loss in water vapor permeation is observed. Possible explanations for this resistance to scaling are presented and discussed. ©2007 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Direct contact membrane distillation (DCMD) is a form of membrane distillation (MD) in which both the liquid feed and liquid permeate are in contact with a porous hydrophobic membrane; a temperature difference between the two solutions gives rise to a transmembrane vapor pressure difference of the volatile species that drives its flux. This DCMD process has now been widely studied in processes in which the major volatile species is water, such as desalination or the concentration of aqueous solutions [1]. Salt rejections of nearly 100% have been reported [1-3]; desalination by DCMD has exhibited fluxes as high as 79 kg/(m 2 h) [2,4,5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since many feedwaters contain sparingly soluble salts, the ability to use DCMD for treating these waters is contingent on DCMDnotbeingsusceptibletoscalingwhenthesesalt solutions become supersaturated. However, as reported in the literature, membrane pores/surfaces may be clogged or membrane pores may get wetted by deposits [6,7]. This may be aggravated by the fact that certain salts, such as CaSO4 (both in anhydrite and gypsum forms) and CaCO3, have an inverse solubility with respect to temperature. There have been reports in the literature of permeate flux decrease due to scaling of MD modules by calcium carbonate [6,8,9] when processing tap water using a module made of polypropylene hollow fibers with the hot feed fluid flowing in the lumen of the fibers. However, to date there has been no systematic assessment of the limits, if any, to DCMD or VMD in terms of CaSO4 scaling.

texts.orig: The induction period, defined as the time elapsed between the creation of supersaturation and the first appearance of a new phase, is an important parameter for nucleation kinetics, and was therefore of significant interest in this study. It was determined by monitoring the variation in the solution calcium concentration with time; the variation of SI as a function of brine inlet temperature and supersaturation was determined. Once calcium sulfate precipitation had occurred, it was then possible to determine if such precipitation had any effect on the performance of the DCMD module vis-` a-vis the water vapor flux, distillate contamination and pressure drop. The scaling experiments were conducted with initial Ca concentrations varying between 18.3-35.0 mmol/l and a brine feed temperature range of 60-90 ◦ C.

texts.orig: Due to both mass and heat transfer effects, the operation of a DCMD unit will cause both concentration and temperature polarization. In the case of the inversely soluble salts (calcium sulfate and calcium carbonate), temperature polarization will tend to reduce the scaling potential at the solution-membrane face while concentration polarization will tend to increase the scaling potential on the membrane-solution surface. In the operation of the hollow fiber crossflow modules (Fig. 1), the brine is fed on the outside of the fibers perpendicular to the fiber axis and the cold distillate is pumped through the lumen of the fibers.

texts.text: 2.3. Presentation of modeling results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The representative results of SI modeling are shown in Figs. 4a and b. The modeling was based on a pilot scale MD module (SN1004) as described in Ref. [12]. This module has 0.29 m 2 internal fiber area (1088 fibers) and linear dimensions of the brine entrance window of 25.5 cm long by 8.6 cm wide. The results are produced for two different distillate temperatures (20 and 75 ◦ C) flowing through the bores of hollow fiber membranes. All other conditions are kept the same for the purpose of comparison. When the fresh hot brine contacts the first layer of hollow fiber membrane module, the coldest distillate temperature will be present at the very entrance of the cold distillate; the distillate will be heated up by the condensing vapor along its flow path. Thus, the driving force at the distillate entrance will be greatest leading to the highest local flux [12]. On the other hand the brine temperature along the fiber length of the first layer is fairly uniform at its inlet value. Since temperature polarization is greatest at the entrance (highest flux), the highest membrane surface temperature will be at the distillate exit end of the fiber [12], and SI will be maximum there if temperature polarization is dominant. If concentration polarization is the dominant effect, the SIs of anhydrite and gypsum should be maximum at the cold distillate entrance location. As can be seen in the results for the first layer in Figs. 4a and b, concentration polarization is the dominant effect. A further concentration polarization effect can be seen in that the SI's in general are lower as the distillate inlet temperature increases for constant brine inlet temperature from Fig. 4a and b. As a result, general flux levels are lower leading to lower concentration polarization.

This module has 0.29 m 2 internal fiber area (1088 fibers) and linear dimensions of the brine entrance window of 25.5 cm long by 8.6 cm wide. The results are produced for two different distillate temperatures (20 and 75 ◦ C) flowing through the bores of hollow fiber membranes. All other conditions are kept the same for the purpose of comparison. When the fresh hot brine contacts the first layer of hollow fiber membrane module, the coldest distillate temperature will be present at the very entrance of the cold distillate; the distillate will be heated up by the condensing vapor along its flow path. Thus, the driving force at the distillate entrance will be greatest leading to the highest local flux [12]. On the other hand the brine temperature along the fiber length of the first layer is fairly uniform at its inlet value. Since temperature polarization is greatest at the entrance (highest flux), the highest membrane surface temperature will be at the distillate exit end of the fiber [12], and SI will be maximum there if temperature polarization is dominant. If concentration polarization is the dominant effect, the SIs of anhydrite and gypsum should be maximum at the cold distillate entrance location. As can be seen in the results for the first layer in Figs. 4a and b, concentration polarization is the dominant effect. A further concentration polarization effect can be seen in that the SI's in general are lower as the distillate inlet temperature increases for constant brine inlet temperature from Fig. 4a and b. As a result, general flux levels are lower leading to lower concentration polarization.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As the brine proceeds to the deeper layers of the module, it will cool down as it progressively releases more vapor to the distillate stream. The depth of the module has been indicated in Fig. 4a and b by % DS (downstream) which is the fraction of the distance from the first fiber layer to the last fiber layer that the mean brine flow has traveled. Thus, the coldest spot of the brine should be near the entrance of the distillate on the last layer of membrane. As a result of this temperature effect, the SI at the fiber entrance drops continuously for both forms of calcium sulfate as the brine flows downstream. The SI profile along the fiber length for the last layer is different for anhydrite and gypsum. In the case of anhydrite, it increases along the fiber length whereas for gypsum it decreases along the fiber length as it does for the first layer. This can be explained as concentration polarization being the controlling phenomenon in the last layer for gypsum whereas temperature polarization is the controlling phenomenon for anhydrite. This is to be expected from the fact that the inverse temperature dependence for anhydrite is greater than it is for gypsum [11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our modeling results provide us the information on where the occurrence of scaling is most likely within the module (near brine inlet or outlet, near distillate inlet or outlet). However, because of the competing influences of temperature polarization and concentration polarization, we still need to know whether the SI will be highest at the membrane surface or in the bulk brine flow. From Figs. 4a and b one can calculate the ratio of the SI at the membrane surface to the SI in the bulk for both anhydrite and gypsum. We found that all along the fiber length the value of this ratio was larger than unity, as shown in Fig. 4c; this implies that the tendency for precipitation of both species is higher at the membrane surface. This result is contrary to the findings of Chernyshov et al. [18] for certain regions in the membranedistillation channel that they modeled. It is likely they were examining conditions with significantly lower fluxes.

texts.orig: Liquid level probes and a level controller (10) connected to a peristaltic pump (11) were used to keep the feed concentration stable. The liquid level inside the brine beaker was maintained between two level probes. If the feed solution level was lower than the low level probe, a certain amount of make-up pure water (9) (noted as m m, g), measured as a weight loss with a digital scale (19) (ACB-plus 1500, Adam, UK), would be pumped into the brine reservoir (14) until the solution level reached the high level probe. Since room-temperature makeup water momentarily cooled down the hot brine solution during the refilling process, the recorded temperature waveform of the brine solution allowed us to determine the water vapor production rate from the time difference between two continuous make-ups (noted as t , min), betweenrefillings since the amount of makeup on each refill, m m, was known: ˙ m v = m m /t . Alternatively it could be calculated from the local slope of the total make-up water weight versus time. For a given period, the overall water vapor production rate ( < ˙ m v > ) can be calculated as < ˙ m v > = m m / t .

texts.orig: 4.1. Gypsum as the precipitating phase
texts.text: 4.1. Gypsum as the precipitating phase

texts.label: text
texts.orig: While CaSO4 crystals come in three polymorphs (anhydrite (CaSO4), hemihydrite (CaSO4 · (1/2)H2O) and gypsum (CaSO4 · 2H2O)), most equilibrium studies have shown that anhydrite is thermodynamically the most stable phase above a temperature of 58 ◦ C[20]. However, XRD analysis of deposit in the brine line from one of the experiments showed only gypsum to be present. In addition, for initial solution concentrations of CaCl2 and Na2SO4 of 26.5 mmol/l (SI(Gyp) = 1.5), the equilibrium concentration at 70 ◦ C will be 14.6 and 19.6 mmol/l for CaSO4 and CaSO4 · 2H2O, respectively, corresponding to a final calcium concentration of 55 and 73.9% of initial concentration for CaSO4 and CaSO4 · 2H2O, respectively. The experimental results show that calcium solution concentration reached an asymptotic value of ∼ 73% (Fig. 6) which matches that for gypsum.
texts.text: While CaSO4 crystals come in three polymorphs (anhydrite (CaSO4), hemihydrite (CaSO4 · (1/2)H2O) and gypsum (CaSO4 · 2H2O)), most equilibrium studies have shown that anhydrite is thermodynamically the most stable phase above a temperature of 58 ◦ C[20]. However, XRD analysis of deposit in the brine line from one of the experiments showed only gypsum to be present. In addition, for initial solution concentrations of CaCl2 and Na2SO4 of 26.5 mmol/l (SI(Gyp) = 1.5), the equilibrium concentration at 70 ◦ C will be 14.6 and 19.6 mmol/l for CaSO4 and CaSO4 · 2H2O, respectively, corresponding to a final calcium concentration of 55 and 73.9% of initial concentration for CaSO4 and CaSO4 · 2H2O, respectively. The experimental results show that calcium solution concentration reached an asymptotic value of ∼ 73% (Fig. 6) which matches that for gypsum.

texts.orig: This result is consistent with other studies that show that gypsum preferentially precipitates even at 90 ◦ C [20-22]. This phenomenoncanbeexplainedasfollows:(1)nucleationofanhydrite is an extremely slow process in comparison to that of gypsum [23] and (2) the interfacial tension between gypsum and solution is lower than that between anhydrite and solutions, because of the higher solubility of gypsum as compared to anhydrite [20].

texts.orig: To determine whether increased calcium precipitation in the solution will decrease the water vapor production rate, in one experiment CaSO4 solution was used as make-up instead of pure water after the induction period (338 min), when [Ca 2+ ] concentration had decreased to 90% of its initial value. The water vapor production rate was stable for the next hour but then began to drop (Fig. 10). The main reason was that the filter holder ((20) in Fig. 5) was blocked by crystals. As a result brine recycle flow rate decreased leading to severe temperature polarization. The experimental loop was even broken as the prefilter was totally blocked by the white precipitate. Since the CaSO4 solution was refilled continually into the pre-seeded hot brine, the crystals grew fast in the solution and particulate fouling may be a dominant mechanism for the blockage on the filter holder. However, except for this extreme case, a filter cake of gypsum particles was never seen to have formed on the filter holder at the end of the scaling experiments (before cleaning).

texts.orig: This work has focused on the potential for scaling in MD of solutions supersaturated in a scaling salt. No attempt has been made to focus on other aspects, such as, energy recovery and energy requirement for successful utilization of DCMD in larger scale. A recent paper published by us [26] has focused on the schematic of energy recovery and the energy requirement for DCMDusingacountercurrent cascade of crossflow hollow fiber distillation modules of the type studied here. Suffice it to say that this modeling study shows that high GOR (gained output ratio) is indeed attainable leading to significant energy efficiency as long as module costs are not excessive. In such a case the cost of water production can be competitive with RO. Our recent laboratory measurements (to be communicated soon) do indeed show that high GORs can be achieved in DCMD with such modules and heat recovery.

For gypsum scaling at the membrane surface, concentration polarization effects are more important than temperature polarization effects.

texts.text: The induction period for the CaSO4 precipitation decreased with increasing feed brine temperature and increasing degree of supersaturation and showed dependencies of classical homogeneous nucleation theory only for higher supersaturations.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Even when there was significant supersaturation in the bulk solution and some precipitation at the membrane surface, there was no significant drop in the permeate production rate.
texts.text: Even when there was significant supersaturation in the bulk solution and some precipitation at the membrane surface, there was no significant drop in the permeate production rate.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. The precipitate formed was found to be gypsum and not anhydrite.
texts.text: The precipitate formed was found to be gypsum and not anhydrite.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. The crossflow hydrodynamics and the porous fluorosilicone coating on the fiber o.d. are both thought to contribute to the resistance of the hollow fibers in such a module to flux decline in the presence of a significant amount of precipitate.
texts.text: The crossflow hydrodynamics and the porous fluorosilicone coating on the fiber o.d. are both thought to contribute to the resistance of the hollow fibers in such a module to flux decline in the presence of a significant amount of precipitate.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. On the other hand, the module must be protected from recycling large amounts of suspended crystalline material which could damage the membrane surface, especially near the potting boundary of the fibers.
texts.text: On the other hand, the module must be protected from recycling large amounts of suspended crystalline material which could damage the membrane surface, especially near the potting boundary of the fibers.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 6. The concentration polarization in such a module was found by modeling to be lower than that in a conventional spiral wound module used in reverse osmosis.
texts.text: The concentration polarization in such a module was found by modeling to be lower than that in a conventional spiral wound module used in reverse osmosis.
